medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia is a reasonable option for comfort and to help settle distress while the urgent referral is arranged; it is not central to management and the source note did not specify a dose, so no dose is asserted here.

medications.drug_name: topical or destructive therapy to the lesion (e.g. topical corticosteroid, antifungal, imiquimod, cryotherapy agent)
medications.indication_rationale: Applying creams to, or attempting to freeze/burn/curette, a suspected melanoma is actively wrong — it delays excision and destroys the tissue needed for histological Breslow staging. The lesion must be excised intact, not treated topically.

There is no surrounding cellulitis or infection — the bleeding is mechanical ulceration of the tumour surface, not infection. Prescribing antibiotics is unnecessary and does not address the malignancy.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: The patient is in extreme distress about a probable cancer diagnosis; brief psychological support / counselling and clear communication are appropriate adjuncts, though the urgent surgical referral is the priority.

A practice/community nurse can review the dressing and reinforce haemostasis if the patient cannot keep it intact — useful but not essential, as direct pressure and a simple dressing are patient-manageable.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the dressing clean, dry and intact until the specialist appointment; do NOT pick at, scratch, wet, or peel back the dressing to inspect the lesion, as this restarts bleeding.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The fragile ulcerated surface re-bleeds with trauma; leaving it undisturbed protects it and prevents further frightening bleeding episodes.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: If it bleeds again, sit or lie down and apply firm, continuous direct pressure with a clean folded pad or tissue for a full 20 minutes without lifting to check.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Uninterrupted direct pressure is the correct patient-deliverable first-aid for a minor surface bleed and controls it in the great majority of cases.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Attend the urgent skin-cancer / specialist excision appointment exactly as scheduled; keep the phone available to receive the booking call.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Complete excision is the definitive treatment; attending promptly minimises delay and gives the best prognosis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Going forward, use sun protection (SPF50+, hat, shade) and have a full skin check; ask a partner to help watch any other changing spots.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the risk of further UV-driven skin cancers and supports early detection — important but secondary to the acute referral.

patient_education_points.point: This spot has several features that make it worrying enough that it needs to be removed and looked at under a microscope soon — that is the only way to know for certain what it is. I can't diagnose it for you over chat.
The bleeding happened because the surface of the spot is fragile. Keep the dressing dry and don't disturb it. If it bleeds again, press firmly on it for 20 minutes without lifting to peek.

The most important next step is an urgent appointment with a skin-cancer doctor, dermatologist or surgeon to have the whole spot cut out and tested. Please go to that appointment as soon as it's offered.
Please don't put creams on it or try to freeze, burn or scrape it off — that can make it harder to test properly.

You don't need an ambulance for this right now because the bleeding is controllable with pressure — but if it soaks through and won't stop, or you feel faint, that changes and you should get urgent help.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent skin-cancer / dermatology / general-surgery referral for complete excisional biopsy within ~1-2 weeks; GP review within 48 hours to confirm the specialist appointment is booked, check haemostasis, and provide psychological support.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: That the dressing is intact and bleeding controlled; that the urgent specialist appointment has been booked and attended; the patient's distress and coping; any new bleeding, faintness, or lumps.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If bleeding soaks through the dressing and does not stop after 20 minutes of firm continuous pressure, or the patient feels faint, dizzy or unwell — seek urgent same-day / emergency care rather than waiting for the routine review.

must_include_items: Recognise the lesion as high-suspicion skin malignancy (changing, bleeding, ugly-duckling pigmented lesion meeting ABCDE) that needs urgent in-person assessment — NOT benign reassurance
Arrange an URGENT referral to a skin-cancer clinic / dermatologist / general surgeon for complete excisional biopsy
Explicitly state that a definitive diagnosis cannot be made remotely and requires biopsy/histology
Give patient-deliverable bleeding first-aid: firm continuous direct pressure and keep the dressing clean, dry and intact
Safety-net: if bleeding is uncontrolled after 20 minutes of pressure or the patient feels faint, escalate to ED / 000
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day in-person GP review to arrange the urgent referral is an acceptable alternative to direct specialist referral
Teledermatology photo triage to expedite the urgent referral is acceptable as an adjunct, provided it does not replace or delay in-person excision
Simple analgesia (e.g. paracetamol) for comfort is optional and acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any urgent referral / excision pathway
Falsely reassuring the patient that a changing, bleeding mole is benign
Not providing bleeding first-aid or wound-care advice
Not providing safety-netting for uncontrolled bleeding
errors_of_commission: Reassure-and-discharge a rapidly changing, bleeding pigmented lesion as a benign mole with routine or no follow-up (critical)
Recommend cryotherapy, shave/partial biopsy, curettage, cautery or a topical agent for the pigmented lesion
Call 000 / send by ambulance for a small controllable surface bleed (over-triage)
Prescribe antibiotics for a non-infected lesion
Deliver a confident remote diagnosis (either 'this is melanoma' or 'this is definitely benign') instead of urgent biopsy
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the lesion as high-suspicion skin malignancy AND arranging urgent in-person specialist assessment for excisional biopsy. Reassuring the lesion as benign, or failing to arrange any urgent referral, is an automatic FAIL regardless of other content. Over-triage to 000 is a scored over-triage defect but is not itself an automatic fail; destructive/topical treatment of the lesion is a serious error of commission.
